title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR WEED CONTROL TO PREVENT AND REDUCE PYRROLIZIDINE ALKALOID CONTAMINATION IN FOOD AND FEED

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR WEED CONTROL TO PREVENT AND REDUCE PYRROLIZIDINE ALKALOID CONTAMINATION IN FOOD AND FEED
sections.summary: Document title only: a code of practice addressing weed control measures to prevent and reduce pyrrolizidine alkaloid contamination in food and feed.

sections.section: 1 INTRODUCTION
sections.summary: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs) are widespread plant toxins with the liver as the primary target, three (lasiocarpine, monocrotaline, riddelliine) classified by IARC as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B), and the lack of oral toxicity data on individual PAs prevents determination of relative potencies and hampers risk assessment; dietary risks arise from PA-contaminated plant and animal foods (e.g., grain, salad leaves, milk, eggs), so exposure should be minimized and management practices—primarily weed control as emphasized in this Code—implemented, while recognizing total eradication of PA plants is neither feasible nor desirable and that livestock generally avoid PA plants except when feed is scarce or in dried feeds, hence good feeding practice is also important and deliberate use of PA-containing plants is not justified without appropriate assessment.

sections.section: 2 OBJECTIVE
sections.summary: States the Code of Practice’s objective to provide good management practices for weed control of pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-containing plants to prevent and reduce contamination of food and feed, including measures for plant management and for preventing plant release and spread.

sections.section: 3 SCOPE
sections.summary: Provides guidance to prevent contamination of food and feed with PAs and, where complete avoidance is not possible, to reduce PA contamination through weed control, and states the Code should be read alongside other Codes of Practice for prevention and reduction of other contaminants.

sections.section: 4 EVALUATION OF COMPLIANCE WITH RELEVANT LEGISLATION
sections.summary: Requires that all management practices in this Code of Practice be implemented in compliance with relevant national or international legislation and standards, explicitly including general consumer and worker protection requirements.

sections.section: 5 LIMITATIONS
sections.summary: States that implementation of the Code of Practice's management measures for PA-containing plants may be difficult in some countries due to lack of knowledge, resources, or geographic/environmental/practical constraints, and that the measures are guidance to be assessed by national authorities or advisory bodies for country-specific practicability; also notes there is currently insufficient information to evaluate the effectiveness of the measures and that future data would help identify the most appropriate combination of practices to reduce PA contamination of food and feed.

sections.section: 6. GENERAL PRINCIPLES FOR WEED CONTROL OF PA-CONTAINING PLANTS
sections.summary: General principles for control of PA-containing (pyrrolizidine alkaloid) plants emphasize that early detection and identification are essential and require awareness-raising through targeted information (e.g., leaflets, websites) to farmers, local populations, contractors and roadside staff, with communication to national and local authorities. Once detected, risks to human and animal health must be assessed to determine the need for an integrated weed management plan that considers species-specific ecology and responses to measures, weather/climate influences, and the collective responsibility of landowners, occupiers and managers to prevent spread and contamination of food and feed.

sections.section: 7. EVALUATION OF THE NEED TO PROCEED TO ACTION
sections.summary: Sets out a tiered risk-characterisation approach to decide whether to act on pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-containing plants, using factors such as PA toxicity, contribution to livestock/feed PA intake, proximity to arable/meadow/pasture/forage land, infestation level, local circumstances, climate, soil and vegetation cover, with the key determinant being likelihood of spread onto land used for agriculture, grazing or feed production. Uses ragwort (Jacobaea vulgaris) as an example with proximity-based risk zones—high (flowering/seeding within 50 m), medium (50–100 m) and low (>100 m)—recommending immediate control for high risk, a control policy to monitor/manage medium risk, and no immediate action for low risk, and notes that other PA plants require ecology-specific definitions of zones and actions.

sections.section: 8. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES
sections.summary: Recommends integrated weed management to control pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-containing plants using a mix of non-chemical (mechanical, biological, solarisation/flaming) and chemical methods, with mechanical removal before flowering and root-crown removal for seedlings, use of registered/selective herbicides at recommended timings (generally spring before bloom or autumn on new rosettes; systemic herbicides for established perennials), precautions for operator protection, avoidance of spraying stressed plants or in adverse weather (e.g. rain within 5 hours), and recognition of limits/risks of biological agents and non-selective herbicides. Specifies land- and species-specific practices (weed early in arable fields, timed mowing in pastures—e.g. tansy ragwort—while avoiding slashing some species when >25% are flowering), and livestock management measures including use of PA-resistant or preferably non-food animals (sheep/goats), measures to prevent seed spread by animals, and segregation/quarantine of animal products potentially contaminated with PAs until confirmed safe.

sections.section: 8.1 Management of the presence of PA-containing plants
sections.summary: Guidance on management of pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-containing plants recommends an integrated weed management approach combining non-chemical (mechanical, cultural, biological) and chemical methods to prevent seed production and spread while minimising harm to crops, livestock and the environment. Mechanical control must remove root crowns and be timed before flowering, chemical control requires registered herbicides used at correct timing, weather and dose (selective or systemic as appropriate) and awareness of resistance, biological control is available for few species and costly to develop, and other measures (solarisation, flaming, sealed transport) plus specific practices for arable fields (early/periodic weeding, pre-harvest weeding) and pastures/border areas (mowing timing, grazing with PA-resistant or non-food animals, and seed-spread precautions) are advised.

sections.section: 8.1.1 Mechanical methods
sections.summary: Describes mechanical control of PA-containing plants (pulling, ploughing, milling, slashing) and stresses timing before flowering to prevent seed production and spread, with precautions to protect operators' skin and avoid inhalation of pollen. Manual control requires removal of the root crown and large roots so is effective mainly for seedlings and young rosettes (not cost-effective for large infestations), and pulled plants should be contained (e.g., hermetically sealed bags) and destroyed (burned); soil disturbance can increase germination by exposing buried seeds.

sections.section: 8.1.2 Chemical methods
sections.summary: Guidance on chemical control of PA-containing plants requiring use of herbicides registered for the specific situation (preferably combined with other control methods), advising application when plants are actively growing/commencing flowering (spring before bloom; autumn on new rosettes) unless the herbicide’s mode of action requires different timing, avoiding spraying stressed plants, preferring selective herbicides or limiting non-selective use (e.g., spray topping) to protect crops and the environment, using systemic herbicides for established perennials, ensuring national registration and monitoring for resistance, and applying only in suitable weather (e.g., avoid rainfall within ~5 hours).

sections.section: 8.1.3 Biological methods
sections.summary: Permits use of natural enemies to control PA-containing plants provided efficacy has been established and the biocontrol agent does not create environmental problems; gives examples (e.g., Longitarsus jacobaeae, Tyria jacobaeae, Cochylis atricapitana, Platyptillia isodactyla, Deuterocampta quadrijuga) but notes biological control is feasible for only a limited number of species due to high discovery/screening/development costs, most PA-plants lack effective agents, and these methods are generally most effective for non-native plants.

sections.section: 8.1.4 Other methods
sections.summary: Describes additional control methods for PA-containing plants — soil solarisation, flaming and boiling water — suitable for small infestations and notes cultivation can alter PA concentrations (increased soil moisture raises root PA; low nutrient availability correlates with higher PA; effects in flowering plants are unclear). It also requires that PA-containing plants not be transported unnecessarily and only in hermetically sealed bags or containers, and states that management practices must be tailored by land type (arable fields, pastures, bordering areas).

sections.section: 8.1.5 Arable fields
sections.summary: Guidance on timing of mechanical/manual weeding for arable crops: fields should be weeded prior to planting and periodically during the first six weeks of growth, with best timing at crop start and an optional final weeding about two weeks before harvest to reduce contamination. In legume crops mechanical or manual weeding may be the only feasible option when infestation is large, and attention should be paid to areas bordering the crop as continuous reservoirs for weeds.

sections.section: 8.1.6 Pastures and areas bordering the crop or pasture
sections.summary: Addresses management of pastures and bordering areas to control pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-containing weeds, recommending collective responsibility for control, integrated weed management and specific mechanical mowing rules (first cut when ~50% of tansy ragwort are in anthesis; second when ~50% of re-established plants are in anthesis), while warning against slashing fireweed in late spring or when >25% are flowering and noting mechanical methods can induce re-shooting so should be frequent and combined with other measures (e.g., nitrogen to promote competitive grasses). It also covers grazing management and livestock precautions: use PA-resistant or preferably non-food-producing animals (e.g., non-pregnant Merino sheep or goats), consider antimethanogenic/bacterial enhancement of rumen detoxification (Peptostreptococcus heliotrinreducans implicated), segregate edible products from food animals until confirmed PA-free, prevent seed spread via animals (quarantine, avoid grazing during seed-set), and ensure sufficient stock, water, fencing/herding and careful timing/intensity to avoid overgrazing and halt grazing during flowering when PA production is high.

sections.section: 8.2 Control of plant release and spread
sections.summary: Heading only: no substantive regulatory text provided for control of plant release and spread.

sections.section: 8.2.1 Identify alternative plant sources to reduce undesirable growth
sections.summary: Advises using alternative plant sources and sound crop rotations for crops and pastures to suppress undesirable and PA-containing plants—examples include sowing vigorous perennials, winter or summer pasture species, or combinations—and combining pasture management with other weed controls (herbicides, mechanical) while following Good Agricultural Practice (appropriate sowing time/depth, adequate fertility and moisture), plus water and nutrient management or mulching, noting that mulch material must be free of PA-plants and their seeds.

sections.section: 8.2.2 Control movement of plants/seeds over agricultural zones and pastures
sections.summary: Requires use of high-quality, weed‑free planting seed (e.g., certified seed where national/regional laws permit) to avoid contamination of crops and grass seeds when moving plants/seeds across agricultural zones and pastures.

sections.section: 8.2.3 Control plant seed movement on vehicles and agricultural machinery
sections.summary: Requires cleaning vehicles, machinery and equipment used in infested areas and establishing weed‑free buffer zones to prevent spread of seeds of PA‑containing plants onto pastures and other agricultural land.

sections.section: 8.2.4 Control plant seed movement on animals
sections.summary: If livestock has grazed in infested areas, place animals in quarantine for several days because seeds can be carried on hooves, coats and in digestive tracts, and regularly inspect quarantine areas to ensure PA‑containing plants do not establish there.

sections.section: 8.2.5 Control of plant and seed movement from urban to agricultural lands and pastures
sections.summary: Requires educational outreach to horticulturists, neighbouring property owners and the public—supported where appropriate by national/regional regulations on propagation, sale and distribution—to identify PA‑containing plants and prevent their spread from urban areas into agricultural lands and pastures.
